what_manipulated: cultural distance of target individual relative to perceiver's own culture
what_measured: objectivism versus relativism in moral judgments
experiment: Six studies presented units with moral scenarios involving targets from their own culture or increasingly distant cultures. Units judged the moral truth-value of actions or statements across these conditions. Analysis tracked the proportion of objectivist versus relativist responses as cultural distance varied. No specific statistical test is detailed; comparisons appear descriptive across the culture-distance manipulation.

what_manipulated: prior engagement with a reflection task; argument strength and deliberation time in a moral scenario
what_measured: utilitarian versus non-utilitarian responses to moral dilemmas
experiment: Experiment 1: units completed a reflection task (Cognitive Reflection Test) before responding to moral dilemmas; utilitarian response rates were compared between high and low reflectiveness conditions. A follow-up associated trait reflectiveness with utilitarian judgment. Experiment 2: units considered a morally charged scenario; a two-factor design crossed argument strength and deliberation time. Analysis examined how these factors and their interaction predicted moral judgment. Statistical tests are not specified in the abstract.

what_measured: college enrollment (4-year vs 2-year vs none) and college persistence
experiment: Longitudinal analysis of N=7,838 adolescents with college enrollment outcome, and subsamples (4-year n=2,605; 2-year n=1,962) with persistence outcomes. Data from the National Longitudinal Survey of Youth 1997. Analysis examined association between adolescent fertility expectations and subsequent college enrollment; pregnancies and college dropout risk were also examined. Statistical methods not specified; the abstract notes rare pregnancy events among college women.

what_manipulated: relationship duration, age, health status, and relationship history
what_measured: presence or absence of sexual activity in past 12 months
experiment: Event-history analysis and logistic regression on N=1,502 married units from a nationally representative sample of 3,005 noninstitutionalized adults aged 57-85 (National Social Life, Health, and Aging Project). Outcome was binary (sexual inactivity past 12 months: 29% reported). Analyses identified independent associations of predictor variables with sexual inactivity; gender differences were also examined.

what_measured: accuracy in phonological perception and production in a second language
experiment: Units (Spanish learners of L2 English and American learners of L2 Spanish) completed a retrieval-induced inhibition task to measure inhibitory control. Phonological accuracy was assessed via speeded ABX categorization (perception) and delayed sentence repetition (production). L2 vocabulary size was measured as a covariate to isolate proficiency effects. Correlational analysis examined relationships between inhibition and segmental perception, consonant production, and vowel production accuracy.

what_manipulated: the default option for organ donor registration status
what_measured: organ donor registration rates and donations
experiment: Natural and experimental data examined policy defaults affecting the decision to become an organ donor. Units were observed or randomly assigned to conditions with different default options (opt-in vs opt-out or similar). Outcome was donor registration rate or donation behavior. Analysis compared registration and donation rates across default conditions.

what_manipulated: induction of moral threat or unethical behavior
what_measured: mental accessibility of cleansing concepts, desire for cleansing products, likelihood of cleansing behavior, and moral self-image threat
experiment: Three studies experimentally induced moral threat. Study 1 examined mental accessibility of cleansing concepts and desire for cleansing products following moral threat. Study 2 measured likelihood of taking antiseptic wipes. Study 3 examined whether physical cleansing alleviates the upsetting consequences of unethical behavior. Analysis tracked increased accessibility, greater product desire, and greater cleansing likelihood in moral-threat conditions; post-cleansing reductions in moral threat were also examined.

what_manipulated: governance rules, communication, and opportunity for costly punishment in a resource-harvesting environment
what_measured: resource harvesting rates and use of costly punishment
experiment: Laboratory experiment with units harvesting resources in an environment with spatial and temporal dynamics mimicking field conditions. A within-subjects design varied governance rules and communication opportunities. Costly punishment was available. Outcome measures were total resource harvesting (gross benefits) and frequency of costly punishment. Analysis compared harvesting rates and punishment use across conditions.

what_measured: postdecisional dissonance effect (attractiveness rating of chosen vs rejected alternative)
experiment: Units made a choice between two alternatives. Half underwent hand washing; half did not. Outcome was the magnitude of postdecisional dissonance—increase in rated attractiveness of chosen alternative and decrease in rejected alternative. Between-subjects design compared dissonance effect in cleansing versus control condition.

what_manipulated: tactile properties of held or touched objects (weight, texture, hardness)
what_measured: impressions and decisions about unrelated people and situations
experiment: Six studies involved units holding heavy or light clipboards, solving rough or smooth puzzles, or touching hard or soft objects while making judgments about unrelated targets (job candidates, social interactions, negotiations). Tactile properties were manipulated between-subjects. Outcomes included importance ratings of job candidates, difficulty judgments of social interactions, and rigidity in negotiation. Analysis examined effects of tactile properties on target judgments.

what_manipulated: presence and beliefs of a social agent
what_measured: reaction times and looking times in a visual detection task
experiment: Adults and 7-month-old infants performed a visual object detection task. An agent with known (but task-irrelevant) beliefs was either present or had previously left the scene. Within-subjects or repeated-measures design compared reaction times (adults) or looking times (infants) when an agent's belief matched the participant's versus when it conflicted. Analysis examined whether agent beliefs modulated response latency independent of participants' own beliefs.

what_manipulated: learning condition: test-restudy versus restudy only
what_measured: accuracy of mediators (information linking cues to targets) and effectiveness in eliciting target responses
experiment: Units studied foreign language translations under two conditions: test-restudy practice versus restudy only. A between-subjects design manipulated learning condition. Outcome measures were retrievability of mediators at test and likelihood that retrieved mediators elicited correct target responses on a final test. Analysis compared mediator quality and performance across conditions.

what_manipulated: number of times a food is imagined to be consumed
what_measured: actual consumption of the imagined food and desire to eat it
experiment: Five studies manipulated imagined consumption frequency. Units repeatedly imagined eating a target food (e.g., cheese) either many times or few times, imagined eating a different food, or did not imagine eating food. A between-subjects design varied imagined consumption. Outcome was actual consumption of the target food at a subsequent test. Analysis examined consumption amount and mediating desire for the food.

what_manipulated: learning activity: retrieval practice versus elaborative studying with concept mapping
what_measured: meaningful learning as assessed by comprehension and inference test questions
experiment: Units studied science texts via retrieval practice or elaborative studying with concept mapping. A between-subjects design manipulated learning activity. Outcomes were performance on final tests assessing comprehension and inference-requiring questions. A within-subjects component tested transfer: final criterion tests used both comprehension questions and concept-map creation. Analysis compared learning gains across study conditions.

what_manipulated: expressive writing about test worries (brief writing assignment immediately before exam)
what_measured: exam performance (score) on a high-stakes test
experiment: Two laboratory and two classroom experiments used randomized assignment to expressive-writing or control condition. Units either wrote about their thoughts regarding an upcoming high-stakes exam or completed a control task immediately before the exam. Outcome was exam score. Subgroup analysis examined whether effects differed for units with high versus low test anxiety. Analysis compared exam performance across conditions and anxiety groups.

what_manipulated: expectation of future information access (availability of search engines)
what_measured: recall of the information itself versus recall of where to access it
experiment: Four studies presented units with difficult questions. In some conditions, units expected future access to information via search; in others they did not. Outcomes were recall accuracy for the information itself and recall for the location or access method. A between-subjects design varied expectation of access. Analysis examined priming effects (increased computer thoughts when facing difficult questions) and subsequent recall patterns.
